params.text:
Recognising a hypo

A hypo can develop within minutes, so knowing the signs means you can act fast. People often describe an early warning feeling, but not everyone gets one, and some people lose their usual warnings over time. This is why your observation matters so much.

  • Physical signs: shaky, sweaty, pale, hungry, dizzy.
  • How they seem: confused, irritable, tearful or unlike themselves.
  • Getting worse: slurred speech, drowsiness, difficulty concentrating.

params.alt: Illustration for Signs of a hypo

params.text:
Signs to watch for

Common signs of a hypo include feeling shaky or trembling, sweating, looking pale, feeling hungry, dizzy or having a fast heartbeat. Just as importantly, a hypo affects how a person thinks and behaves: they may become confused, irritable, tearful, anxious or simply not themselves. Someone who is usually calm may become cross, and someone chatty may go quiet.

Because these behaviour changes can be mistaken for a bad mood, tiredness or, in older people, confusion, it is important to think “could this be a hypo?” whenever someone with diabetes suddenly seems different. If in doubt, treat it as a hypo and check their blood glucose if that is part of the plan. It is always safer to treat a suspected hypo than to ignore it.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
params.textField: A hypo is when blood glucose drops below *4* mmol/L. The person may feel *shaky* and *sweaty*, look *pale*, and become *confused* or irritable.
